hints: Each stop maps x → x/4 + 7; apply it 3 times.
After 3 stops the count is (x+588)/64; need it integer with all stages integer.
solution: Each stop sends $x\mapsto\tfrac x4+7$. After three stops the count is $\tfrac{x}{64}+\tfrac{147}{16}=\tfrac{x+588}{64}$. Taking $x=52$ keeps every stage integral: $52\to20\to12\to10$. The smallest possible final count is $10.$

hints: No rain = dry both days; use independence.
Write N(15)=(N(15)−N(5))+N(5) and use independent increments.
Range=|X₁−X₂|, midpoint=(X₁+X₂)/2; the condition becomes max > 3·min.
solution: The range is $O_2-O_1$ and the midpoint $\tfrac{X_1+X_2}{2}=\tfrac{O_1+O_2}{2}$; the condition $O_2-O_1>\tfrac{O_1+O_2}{2}$ simplifies to $O_2>3O_1$, i.e. $\max>3\min$. By symmetry $P=2P(X_2>3X_1)=2\int_0^\infty e^{-x}e^{-3x}dx=2\cdot\tfrac14=\tfrac12.$

hints: The replacement is a uniform card from the 48 not originally selected.
By symmetry that card is marginally uniform over the deck.
solution: The replacement card is drawn uniformly from the $48$ cards not initially selected. By symmetry over which $4$ were selected, the replacement is marginally a uniformly random card of the deck, so its expected rank is $\tfrac{1+2+\dots+13}{13}=7.$

hints: Expected doors opened to reach the first of 2 'money' doors among 7 is (7+1)/(2+1) (First-Ace).
= 8/3 openings, each costing x; fair means cost = $200.
solution: By the 'First Ace' argument, the $2$ money doors split the $5$ empties into $3$ gaps, so the expected number of doors opened to hit the first money door is $\tfrac{7+1}{2+1}=\tfrac83$. Fairness requires $x\cdot\tfrac83=200$, so $x=75.$

hints: Focus on the center sub-cube of the 3×3×3.
Its 6 faces are all interior, each requiring a separate cut (repositioning can't create two opposite faces in one cut).
solution: Consider the central $1\times1\times1$ cube. Each of its $6$ faces is an internal surface that must be produced by a distinct cut (no single planar cut can form two opposite faces of it, even with rearranging). Hence at least $6$ cuts are needed, and $6$ (two per axis) suffice.

hints: Mean of U(0,λ) is λ/2; mean of Exp(λ) is 1/λ.
x̄=0, ȳ=1.5. Compute S_xy and S_xx.
solution: Here $\bar x=0,\bar y=1.5$. Then $S_{xy}=\sum x_i(y_i-\bar y)=-6$ and $S_{xx}=\sum x_i^2=10$, so $\hat\beta_1=-0.6$ and $\hat\beta_0=\bar y-\hat\beta_1\bar x=1.5$. Their sum is $1.5-0.6=0.9.$

hints: P(stop before 234x)=x; profit is −2x then, +x otherwise.

solution: With $E[Y\mid X]=\tfrac{X+1}2$ and $\mathrm{Var}(Y\mid X)=\tfrac{(1-X)^2}{12}$: $E[Y]=E[\tfrac{X+1}2]=\tfrac34$, and $\mathrm{Var}(Y)=E\big[\tfrac{(1-X)^2}{12}\big]+\mathrm{Var}\big(\tfrac{X+1}2\big)=\tfrac1{36}+\tfrac1{48}=\tfrac{7}{144}$. Hence $\dfrac{E[Y]}{\mathrm{Var}(Y)}=\dfrac{3/4}{7/144}=\dfrac{108}{7}.$

hints: Each of the 10 tiles produces an elimination (first attempt wrong) independently w.p. 1/2.
Contestant i wins iff exactly i−1 eliminations occur: p_i = C(10,i−1)/2^10.
solution: Each tile causes exactly one elimination if its first guess is wrong (probability $\tfrac12$), independently across the $10$ tiles. Contestant $i$ wins iff there are exactly $i-1$ eliminations, so $p_i=\dfrac{\binom{10}{i-1}}{2^{10}}$. This is largest at $i-1=5$: $\dfrac{\binom{10}{5}}{1024}=\dfrac{252}{1024}=\dfrac{63}{256}.$
